metadata.title: Expand Your Strongest Arc to 300 Words
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 9 brief — Making Your Brand Unforgettable

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 3 of 6. Part 1 also requires a 300-word expanded story — your strongest arc from Episode 2, opened up into something that could live on your About page. Three hundred words is space for a full five-point structure: setup, rising action, turning point, resolution, and takeaway. The takeaway is the most important part and the most commonly missed. Let's go.

props.segment: Expand Your Strongest Arc to 300 Words
props.command: Pick the strongest 100-word arc → expand to ~300 words using the 5-point structure. Your voice, not a brand voice.
props.runningText: takeaway is for the reader, not for you — the most commonly missed element…
output: 5-point structure: Setup · Rising Action · Turning Point · Resolution · Takeaway
Takeaway is about the reader, not a summary of what you did

beats.narration_text: The five-point structure. Setup: the scene, the challenge, or the opportunity. Not a biography — a specific context that puts the reader somewhere. 'It was week three of the pipeline build and the record counts were off by about thirty percent every morning' puts the reader in a room. 'I've always been interested in data quality' does not. Rising Action: what happened and what was at stake. The complication, the attempt, the escalation. This is where the specific details from your bullets live — not a summary of effort, but the actual things that happened in sequence. Turning Point: the decision or the insight that changed everything. The sentence that is the spine of the whole story. One sentence. If you have two candidates for the turning point, one of them is setup for the other — pick the one later in the sequence. Resolution: what changed. What you built, what you fixed, what you learned. This is concrete — not 'I grew from the experience' but 'the pipeline ran cleanly for the first time and the daily report matched the source count.' Takeaway: what it means for the reader or user. This is the most important part and the most commonly missed. The takeaway is not a summary of what you did — it is what someone reading this can carry forward. 'If you are building a data pipeline and your counts do not match, check whether the quality gate criteria still match the brief — not just whether the gate ran.'

props.artifactHeading: Setup · Rising Action · Turning Point · Resolution · Takeaway
artifactLines: Setup: specific context that puts the reader somewhere — not 'I've always been interested in X.'
Rising Action: what happened in sequence, with specific details — not 'I worked hard.'
Turning Point: one sentence. If you have 2 candidates, pick the later one.
Resolution: what changed — concrete, not 'I grew from the experience.'
Takeaway: what the READER carries forward. About them, not you. Most commonly missed.
props.sparkLine: Takeaway is for the reader. That's the part they remember. Not your summary.

beats.act: the voice pass — your voice, not a brand voice
beats.narration_text: The assignment brief says this directly: write it in your own voice — not the voice you think a brand should have. This is worth pausing on. The voice you think a brand should have is polished, confident, slightly formal, and sounds like it could be a company press release. Your actual voice has contractions, incomplete thoughts, and the specific way you describe things that nobody else would describe that way. That is the one to use. After Claude expands the arc into 300 words, read it paragraph by paragraph out loud. Two tests. First: could this paragraph have been written by anyone? If yes, rewrite it with a specific detail that could only come from you — a number, a name, a tool you actually used, a moment that happened in your actual project. Second: does any sentence use a word you would never actually say? Substitute the word you would say. Not 'leveraged the data pipeline capabilities' — 'used the pipeline.' Not 'demonstrated resilience in the face of challenges' — 'the pipeline broke three times in the first week and I fixed it.' The 300-word story is the piece most likely to live on your About page, a LinkedIn summary, or a portfolio introduction. The person who sounds like a real person in that space stands out. The person who sounds like a brand template does not.

props.artifactTitle: Voice pass — your voice, not a brand voice
artifactLines: Test 1: could this paragraph be written by anyone? If yes — add a specific detail only you have.
Test 2: does any sentence use a word you'd never say? Substitute the word you'd actually use.
'Leveraged the data pipeline capabilities' → 'used the pipeline.'
'Demonstrated resilience' → 'the pipeline broke 3 times the first week and I fixed it.'
The person who sounds like a real person in an About page stands out. Template = invisible.
props.sparkLine: Your voice has specific details nobody else has. Use them. That's the whole assignment.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for the 300-Word Expanded Story. Pick the strongest arc from Episode 2. Expand it using the five-point structure: setup puts the reader somewhere specific, rising action names what happened in sequence, turning point is one sentence — pick the later candidate if you have two — resolution is concrete, and the takeaway is for the reader, not a summary of what you did. Apply the voice pass: every paragraph that could be written by anyone needs a specific detail; every word you would never say needs to be replaced with the word you would say. Your voice, not a brand voice. The 300-word story is the one most likely to live on your About page. Episode 4 turns the strongest arc into the 500-word article.

props.artifactTitle: Expand Your Strongest Arc to 300 Words
artifactLines: 5 points: Setup · Rising Action · Turning Point · Resolution · Takeaway.
Turning point = one sentence (later of two candidates).
Takeaway = for the reader, not a summary of you.
Voice pass: specific detail in every paragraph · no words you'd never actually say.
This piece lives on your About page — real voice stands out, template is invisible.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the expansion prompt. Pick the strongest of your three arcs. Paste it into Claude with this prompt. The prompt reads: I want to expand this 100-word arc into a 300-word story using the five-point structure. The arc is: [paste your best 100-word arc]. The five points I want to build are: Setup — the specific scene or context: [2–3 bullet points of setup details you have]. Rising Action — what happened and what was at stake: [3–5 bullet points of the sequence]. Turning Point — this is the sentence I want to keep exactly: [paste it]. Resolution — what changed: [1–2 bullet points]. Takeaway — what a reader can carry forward from this story: [one sentence that is about the reader, not you]. Expand into approximately 300 words, staying inside these five sections. Do not add details I have not given you. Do not use template language. Write in the first person, in a direct voice. After you get the draft, apply the voice pass: read every paragraph out loud, mark the words you would never say, and rewrite those sentences in your own words. The story is not done until you can read all 300 words aloud and hear yourself, not a press release.

props.segment: Expand Your Strongest Arc to 300 Words
props.command: Arc: [paste best 100-word arc]. Setup bullets: [2–3]. Rising action bullets: [3–5]. Turning point: [paste exact sentence]. Resolution bullets: [1–2]. Takeaway: [reader-facing sentence]. Expand to ~300 words. No added details. No template language.
props.runningText: story is not done until you can read all 300 words aloud and hear yourself, not a press release…

beats.narration_text: Expand Your Strongest Arc to 300 Words. Liam, in for Bear.
